claim: A 2023 systematic review found that fewer than 10 percent of smallholder farmers in Sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia use improved climate-resilient seed varieties, with access constrained by seed-multiplication capacity, distribution-network reach, farmer awareness, and affordability.

source.title: Seed systems and climate-resilient agriculture in LMICs

method: Reviewed seed-system analysis, adoption-rate studies, barrier classification, and geographic coverage. Focused on the 10 percent figure methodology and seed-system bottleneck evidence.
limitations: Adoption data varies substantially by crop, country, and survey methodology — the 10 percent figure is an aggregate that masks wide variation.
Seed-system data is fragmented across national agricultural surveys, CGIAR project reports, and private-sector data that is not publicly available.
The review focuses on adoption rates, not on-farm yield performance — adoption does not guarantee yield advantage under farmer conditions.
The 10 percent figure may be outdated for specific crops and countries where recent seed-system investments have improved access.

claim: CGIAR centers (CIMMYT, IRRI, ICRISAT, CIAT) and national agricultural research systems have released hundreds of climate-resilient varieties over the past two decades — drought-tolerant maize, flood-tolerant rice, heat-tolerant wheat, early-maturing legumes — but seed-system bottlenecks mean that varieties reach a fraction of their target farmers.
method: Reviewed CGIAR center documentation for climate-resilient variety releases, target geographies, and seed-system delivery challenges.
limitations: CGIAR documentation emphasizes variety releases, not adoption outcomes — the number of released varieties does not indicate how many farmers actually plant them.
Seed-system delivery data is reported at project level, not systematically aggregated across centers or countries.
Variety-release catalogs may not reflect current availability — varieties may be released but not actively multiplied or distributed.
CGIAR centers do not typically track farmer-level adoption; that data comes from national agricultural surveys and project evaluations.

claim: The African Seed Access Index (TASAI) tracks seed-sector development indicators — variety release, seed production, quality control, market access — across 20+ African countries, providing the most systematic cross-country seed-system data available.
method: Reviewed TASAI methodology for seed-sector indicator construction, country coverage, and data-access conditions.
limitations: TASAI covers African countries only — South Asian seed-system data requires different sources.
TASAI indicators are national-level aggregates that may not capture sub-national variation in seed availability.
The index measures seed-sector infrastructure, not farmer-level adoption — good TASAI scores do not guarantee farmer access.
TASAI data is updated periodically; some country assessments may reflect conditions from several years prior.

claim: The difference between on-station yield trials (where new varieties are tested under optimal management) and on-farm yields (where farmers face input, labor, and knowledge constraints) can be 30-50 percent, meaning that adoption is not just about seed access but about the complementary inputs and extension that make new varieties perform.

source.title: Bridging the yield gap: from on-station to on-farm performance of improved varieties

method: Reviewed yield-gap analysis methodology, crop-country case studies, and on-farm trial design assessment.
limitations: The 30-50 percent yield gap is a range across multiple crops and countries — specific crop-country combinations may have larger or smaller gaps.
Yield-gap estimates depend on the comparison methodology — which on-station trials and which on-farm sites are selected affects the gap.
The gap may narrow as farmers gain experience with new varieties and as extension services improve — the figure represents a snapshot, not a permanent barrier.
Complementary input access (fertilizer, water, labor) varies by farmer wealth and location — the yield gap may be larger for resource-poor farmers.
